did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a convolutional neural network (CNN) to automate the recognition and detection of atypical epidermotropic and dermal lymphocytes in H&E-stained skin sections from patients with cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (MF). Expert clinicians labeled images to train the AI model, which was then applied to independent testing datasets from both brightfield and nonlinear optical microscopy (NLOM) to assess performance in detecting characteristic features of skin T-lymphocytes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the use of nonlinear optical microscopy (NLOM) and a convolutional neural network (CNN) to analyze H&E-stained biopsy samples from 10 patients with mycosis fungoides. It includes specific sections for Methods, Results, and Conclusions, describing the training of an AI model and the assessment of its performance on independent testing datasets, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Multi-Harmonic Imaging-Based Automated Recognition of Cutaneous T-Cell Lymphoma
brief_summary: This study addresses the diagnostic challenges of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL), specifically mycosis fungoides, by integrating nonlinear optical microscopy (NLOM) with artificial intelligence. The researchers utilized H&E-stained biopsy samples from ten patients with early-stage MF, alongside fresh unstained biopsies, to train a convolutional neural network (CNN) for automated lymphocyte detection. Expert dermatopathologists labeled images to train the model, which was then evaluated on independent testing datasets from both brightfield and NLOM modalities. The AI model achieved an F1 score approaching 0.8 on NLOM images, demonstrating slightly higher precision and consistency compared to brightfield-based detection. Additionally, NLOM successfully visualized sub-cellular structures in fresh, unstained tissue up to 400 µm deep, highlighting its potential for non-invasive in vivo diagnostics. The findings suggest that combining NLOM with AI offers a powerful tool to improve the accuracy and timeliness of CTCL diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused on H&E-stained biopsy samples from 10 patients with early-stage mycosis fungoides (MF), a type of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL). The AI model was trained to detect characteristic features (atypical epidermotropic and dermal lymphocytes) specific to MF lesions.
Ten healthy skin samples were included in the study alongside the MF samples, likely serving as a control or baseline for comparison in the imaging and analysis.

all_task_types.target: Atypical epidermotropic and dermal lymphocytes in H&E-stained skin sections.
all_task_types.details: The study trained a convolutional neural network (CNN) to detect and localize specific cells (lymphocytes) in images. The model output binary layers for true positives, false positives, and false negatives, and performance was evaluated using an F1 score. While the task involves identifying a specific class of object (lymphocytes vs. other cells), the primary AI function described is automated detection/localization of these features within the tissue images.

datasets_used.name: CHUV Dermatology Biobank (CHUV_2103_12)
datasets_used.description: A VITA-certified Dermatology Biobank at the Lausanne University Hospital (CHUV) providing formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) clinical samples and fresh skin biopsies.

use.training: H&E-stained biopsy samples from 10 MF patients were used to train a CNN model for lymphocyte detection. Expert clinicians labeled images for training.
use.testing: An independent testing dataset was prepared from the biobank samples (specifically mentioned as 5 test images corresponding to 685 lymphocytes) to assess the AI model's performance.
use.other: Fresh, unstained biopsy samples from the biobank were used to demonstrate NLOM's potential for in vivo imaging up to 400 µm deep.

statistical_methods_used.method: Statistical analysis of morphological features (maximum Feret’s diameter and fill area)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the size distributions of skin lymphocytes versus other cells present in the images, determining if skin lymphocytes are statistically smaller.

To evaluate and optimize the performance of the convolutional neural network (CNN) model for automated lymphocyte detection, assessing the trade-off between precision and recall across different training dataset sizes and iteration numbers.
To visualize data dispersion and highlight statistical differences in morphological features between skin lymphocytes and other cells.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical analysis of morphological features (maximum Feret’s diameter and fill area) using boxplot analysis to compare distributions.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the size distributions (maximum Feret’s diameter and fill area) between skin lymphocytes and other cells present in the images.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: Skin lymphocytes are statistically smaller than other cells present in the images.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The boxplot analysis highlighted data dispersion, showing that skin lymphocytes are statistically smaller than the other cells. The paper notes 'see Supplementary for statistics' regarding the specific p-values or test statistics, but does not report the exact alpha threshold or p-values in the main text.

NLOM_Testing_Set.description: Independent testing dataset from NLOM images (subset of 5 images corresponding to 685 lymphocytes, later scaled to 22 images/2944 lymphocytes)

CNN (optimized).notes: Performance approached a plateau slightly below F1 = 0.8. The model detected lymphocyte epidermotropism and dermal infiltration. True positives constituted the majority of detections, with non-negligible false positives and false negatives.

NLOM_Testing_Set.notes: The F1 score positively correlated with increasing training image size, plateauing below 0.8. Performance was conserved when scaling from a subset of 5 to 22 images.

Brightfield_Testing_Set.description: Validation set of 20 images from conventional brightfield H&E-stained histological images
Brightfield_Testing_Set.notes: NLOM with AI demonstrated slightly higher precision and improved F1 score compared to the brightfield-based model, although the difference did not reach statistical significance on all three parameters. The NLOM dataset exhibited lower variability in performance across different test images.

Morphological_Analysis.description: Statistical analysis of cell features (maximum Feret’s diameter and fill area) for skin lymphocytes vs. other cells
Morphological_Analysis.notes: Skin lymphocytes were found to be statistically smaller than other cells present in the images. Objects smaller than 6 μ² (fill area) and 3 μm (maximum Feret’s diameter) were excluded as incomplete segments or debris.

Complementary_Model_Validation.description: Validation using an independent complementary model trained to detect 'other cells' (non-lymphocytes)
Complementary_Model_Validation.notes: 263 TP lymphocytes were misclassified by the complementary model, indicating limited overlap with the lymphocyte model and emphasizing that NLOM morphological and spectral properties are reliable features for identification.

architecture_used.input_size: 835 × 835 pixels (8-bit RGB) for brightfield; NLOM raw images (dimensions not explicitly specified for NLOM input tiles, but whole-slide images were stitched from high-resolution tiles)

architecture_details.implementation_details: Trained using human-assisted segmentation binary cell layers. Expert dermatopathologists identified and labelled target cells based on cross-verification with corresponding brightfield H&E-stained slide images. The model was iteratively supervised and refined by correcting false-positive and false-negative detections.
architecture_details.software: NIS-Elements Advanced Research software (Version 5.42.07, Nikon, Tokyo, Japan) was used for the DetectionClasses classifier application alongside the CNN model.

training_details.training_data_size: 1963 lymphocytes (25 images) for the final optimized model; tested ranges from 250 to 2349 lymphocytes (3 to 30 images). For brightfield comparison, 23 cropped image fields were used.
training_details.hyperparameters: Exclusion size-based criterion: objects smaller than 6 μm² (fill area) and 3 μm (maximum Feret’s diameter) were excluded.
training_details.training_schedule: Iterative supervised refinement to improve F1 score.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Binary layers for true positives (TP), false positives (FP), and false negatives (FN)
classes: Other cells (non-lymphocytes, detected by a separate complementary model)

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping of regions from whole-slide images for AI annotation and testing
Exclusion of automatically segmented objects smaller than 6 μm² (fill area) and 3 μm (maximum Feret’s diameter) to remove incomplete segments, partial cells, or debris
Stitching of high-resolution, small field-of-view tiles to generate whole-slide images for brightfield microscopy
conclusions: Nonlinear optical microscopy (NLOM) combined with artificial intelligence (AI) can successfully detect lymphocyte epidermotropism and dermal infiltration in mycosis fungoides (MF) H&E-stained skin tissue.
The integration of AI with NLOM offers dermatologists a powerful tool to improve the diagnosis and prognosis of MF-CTCL, potentially enabling more timely and precise therapeutic strategies.
NLOM can visualize epidermal and dermal cells and extracellular skin structures with sub-cellular resolution in both stained and unstained samples.
NLOM has the potential to enable non-invasive diagnostics by imaging specific tissue structures with label-free contrast.

The AI-driven NLOM approach demonstrated slightly higher precision and an improved F1 score compared to brightfield-based models, with lower variability in performance across test images, although the difference did not reach statistical significance on all parameters.
NLOM can image fresh, unstained biopsies up to 400 µm deep through the epidermis to the dermis, highlighting its potential for in vivo skin imaging.
The study highlights the feasibility of integrating nonlinear imaging modalities into existing AI pipelines without compromising detection accuracy.

Clinical implementation of NLOM will require further development to address current limitations, such as the need for specialized software solutions designed specifically for dermatology applications.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size, utilizing only 10 MF patients and 10 healthy controls from a single biobank (CHUV), which may introduce population bias and limit generalizability.

The difference in performance between the NLOM-AI model (F1=0.798) and the brightfield-AI model (F1=0.698) did not reach statistical significance on all parameters, suggesting the improvement may not be robust.

The AI model's performance plateaued slightly below an F1 score of 0.8, indicating that while promising, the automated detection is not yet perfect and retains a non-negligible rate of false positives and false negatives.

The NLOM imaging was performed on fresh, unstained biopsies ex vivo to demonstrate depth capability (up to 400 µm); clinical in vivo implementation requires further development of specialized dermatology software solutions.
The study acknowledges that histological features of early MF can be masked by prior topical steroid use, a common clinical confounder not explicitly controlled for in the imaging analysis.
Data availability is restricted to 'reasonable request' rather than open access, which may hinder independent replication and external validation of the AI model.
